items.correct: No - 3D printing is additive: it deposits material layer by layer, whereas subtractive processes cut material away from a solid block.
items.misconception: Yes - 3D printers carve the shape out of a block of plastic.
items.random_wrong: Yes - 3D printing is a type of injection moulding.
items.why: Students picture all machines as cutting tools. The defining feature of additive manufacture is building up material from nothing, which also minimises waste.

items.q: Is mass production always the cheapest way to make a product?
items.correct: No - mass production gives a low unit cost only at high volumes; for small quantities, the huge tooling and set-up costs make one-off or batch production cheaper overall.
items.misconception: Yes - mass production is always cheaper than any other scale.
items.random_wrong: Yes - because mass-produced items never need quality checks.
items.why: Unit cost and total cost get confused. Expensive moulds and lines only pay off when spread across very many units; below that break-even volume they are wasted money.

items.q: Do robots in automated production remove the need for human workers entirely?
items.correct: No - humans are still needed to program, supervise and maintain robots, manage quality systems and handle non-routine problems.
items.misconception: Yes - a fully automated factory needs no human involvement at all.
items.random_wrong: Yes - robots also design the products they make.
items.why: Automation shifts labour rather than abolishing it: routine manual tasks fall, while skilled technical roles in programming and maintenance grow.

items.q: Which of these is an additive manufacturing technique?
items.correct: 3D printing a part layer by layer
items.random_wrong: Turning a bar on a lathe
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3D printing a part layer by layer. “Milling on a CNC machine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Turning a component on a lathe is an example of:
items.why: It's Subtractive (wasting) manufacturing. “Additive manufacturing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a typical characteristic of one-off production?
items.correct: A single unique product made by skilled labour at high unit cost
items.random_wrong: Thousands of identical products per day
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A single unique product made by skilled labour at high unit cost. “Very low cost per unit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A key advantage of using robots on a production line is:
items.correct: Consistent, repeatable accuracy on repetitive tasks
items.misconception: They never require programming or maintenance
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Consistent, repeatable accuracy on repetitive tasks. “They never require programming or maintenance” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A mass production line makes 120 units per hour. How many units does it make in an 8-hour shift?
items.why: Exact answer: 960 units. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
